zh.lifeSummary: Albert "Juvenis" de Grelle, Baron of Manchester是BARON OF MANCHESTER ROBERT DE GRESLEY的兒子，於1120年出生在英國藍卡爾曼徹斯特。他和Isabel Basset至少有1個兒子和1個女兒。他去世於大約1180年在英國英格蘭藍卡爾道爾頓享年61歲。

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Grelle,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: German: variant of   Grell  .

variants.name: Grell
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Hans, Kurt, Claus, Dietrich, Franz, Guenter.
German: nickname for an irritable, irascible person, from Middle High German grel ‘rough, angry’.
German: metonymic occupational name from Middle High German grelle ‘spiked instrument; fork, spear’ for a user or maker.

nameMeanings.name: Baron
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: French, English, Dutch, Polish, Czech, Romanian, and Croatian; Breton (also Le Baron): from Old French barun, baron, Middle English barun, baroun, Middle Dutch baroen, Polish, Czech, Romanian, and dialectal Croatian baron ‘baron’ (i.e. a nobleman ranking below an earl and above a knight), a title of nobility which was in England also awarded to certain freemen of the cities of London and York and of the Cinque Ports. As a surname it is unlikely to be a status name denoting a person of rank. Generally, it referred to service in a baronial household or was acquired as a nickname by a peasant who had ideas above his station or applied to a man showing characteristics of a nobleman. See also 3 and 4 below, compare Barron and Lebaron .
French: habitational name from (Le) Baron, the name of several places in various parts of France. Compare   Lebaron  .
German: status name for a freeman or baron, from barūn ‘imperial or church official’, a loanword in Middle High German from Old French (see 1 above).

variants.name: Barron
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Juan, Jesus, Manuel, Francisco, Carlos, Miguel, Raul, Pedro, Ruben, Alfredo, Luis.

Scottish: status name from Scots baron, which in the Highlands denoted a small proprietary landholder. In Angus it alluded specifically to a tenant of one of the small baronies attached to the Abbey of Coupar Angus. The surname was taken by Scottish migrants to Ulster. Compare Baron .
Scottish: shortened form of McBarron, from Scottish Gaelic Mac a' Bharain ‘son of the baron’. See Baron and 1 above.

variants.name: Boron
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Polish Krystyna, Edyta, Leszek, Mieczyslaw, Mieczyslawa, Wladyslaw.
Polish (Boroń): from a pet form of the personal names Borzysław or Borzymir, formed with the element bor ‘to fight’ (from Old Slavic boriti se).
French: habitational name from a place so called in Franche-Comté.

variants.name: Baro
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Mario, Manuel, Ana, Angel, Carlos, Cesar, Fernando, Jesus, Jose, Orlando, Ramon, Rosalia.  Italian Guido, Angelo, Carmelo, Federico, Lucio.
Catalan (Baró): status name from baró ‘free man, baron’ (see Baron 1), or a habitational name from a place so named.
Hungarian (Báró): status name from báró ‘baron’. In the form Baró, it could be from a pet form of the personal names Barabás (see Barabas ) or Bertalan.

variants.name: Barona
meanings: Spanish: from the feminine form of Barón ‘baron’ (see Baron ), applied as a nickname or as an occupational name for a member of the household of a baroness.
Italian: probably from the feminine form of   Barone  . The name is concentrated in Sondrio province (Lombardy).

variants.name: Barren
meanings: English: probably a variant of   Baron   or   Barran  .

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: German: possibly a metonymic occupational name for a fisherman, from orfe, a species of freshwater fish. Alternatively, a habitational name from Urff near Kassel, formerly Orpha.

variants.name: Orff
meanings: German: variant of   Orf  .

nameMeanings.name: Manchester
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: 1 English: habitational name from a place in Nottinghamshire. The early forms, from Domesday Book to the early 13th century, show the first element uniformly as Mam-, and it is therefore likely that this was a British hill-name meaning ‘breast’ ( compare Manchester ), with the later addition of Old English feld ‘pasture’, ‘open country’ ( see Field ) as the second element. The surname is now widespread throughout Midland and southern England and is also common in Ireland.
2  Irish: when not an importation of 1, this is an altered form of the Norman name Manville (  see  Mandeville  ).
3  Americanized form of German and Jewish (Ashkenazic) Mansfeld, a habitational name for someone from a place so called in Saxony.

variants.name: Field
meanings: English and Irish: habitational name, probably from Field, in Leigh, Staffordshire. The placename derives from Old English feld ‘flat open country’. In the late 12th century one of Henry II's warrior knights took the surname to Ireland, where it often took the semi-Norman French form de la Feld. From the 15th century onward it was increasingly reduced to Field and gave its name to Fieldstown, the family's chief seat near Dublin. A branch of the Anglo-Irish family that migrated back to England in the 14th century retained the Normanized form as Delafield .
English: topographic name for someone who lived by an arable field or an area of open country (Middle English feld).
Irish: Anglicized form of Feeley , through similarity of sound, and of Maghery by translation (chiefly in Armagh), from Gaelic An Mhachaire ‘of the field’.

variants.name: Mandeville
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Marcel, Andre, Armand, Gabrielle, Camille, Emile, Fernand, Fernande, Gaston, Jean-Paul, Lucien, Michel.

French: habitational name from Mandeville, the name of two places in Normandy, derived from Latin magna villa ‘large estate’, or a variant of Manneville, a habitational name from Manneville, the name of several places in Normandy (see 2 below). Compare Montville and Monville .
English (of Norman origin): habitational name from Manneville (Seine-Maritime, Calvados), Manneville-sur-Risle (Eure), or Magneville (Manche).

nameMeanings.name: Albert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From an Old French name, Albert, of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from adal ‘noble’ + berht ‘bright, famous’. This was adopted by the Normans and introduced by them to England, displacing the Old English form Æþelbeorht. The name is popular in a variety of forms in Western Europe, and has been traditional in a number of European princely families. It was out of favour in England for centuries, however, and the revival of its popularity in the 19th century was largely in honour of Queen Victoria's consort, Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg-Gotha .

en.lifeSummary: Albert "Juvenis" de Grelle, Baron of Manchester was born in 1120, in Manchester, Lancashire, England as the son of BARON OF MANCHESTER ROBERT DE GRESLEY. He had at least 1 son and 1 daughter with Isabel Basset. He died about 1180, in Dalton, Lancashire, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 61.

fr.lifeSummary: Albert "Juvenis" de Grelle, Baron of Manchester est né en 1120, à Manchester, Lancashire, Angleterre, fils de BARON OF MANCHESTER ROBERT DE GRESLEY. Il a eu au moins 1 fils et 1 fille avec Isabel Basset. Il est décédé environ 1180, à Dalton, Lancashire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 61 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Albert "Juvenis" de Grelle, Baron of Manchester wurde 1120 in Manchester, Lancashire, England, als Sohn von BARON OF MANCHESTER ROBERT DE GRESLEY geboren. Er hatte mit Isabel Basset mindestens 1 Sohn und 1 Tochter. Er starb um 1180 im Alter von 61 Jahren in Dalton, Lancashire, England, United Kingdom.

it.lifeSummary: Albert "Juvenis" de Grelle, Baron of Manchester è nato il nel 1120 nel Manchester, Lancashire, England come figlio di BARON OF MANCHESTER ROBERT DE GRESLEY. Lui avuto almeno 1 figlio e 1 figlia con Isabel Basset. Morì circa 1180, nel Dalton, Lancashire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito, all'età di 61 anni.

ko.lifeSummary: Albert "Juvenis" de Grelle, Baron of Manchester(은)는 1120년 영국 랭카실르 먼체스터에서 BARON OF MANCHESTER ROBERT DE GRESLEY의 아들로 태어났다. 그는 Isabel Basset(와)과 적어도 한 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 대략 1180년 영국 잉글랜드 랭카실르 돌턴에서 61세의 나이로 사망했다.

ru.lifeSummary: Albert "Juvenis" de Grelle, Baron of Manchester родился в 1120 г. в местности манчестир, ланкашир, Англия. Его отца звали BARON OF MANCHESTER ROBERT DE GRESLEY. У него был по крайней мере 1 сын и 1 дочь от Isabel Basset. Он умер около 1180 г. в местности далтон, ланкашир, Англия, Великобритания в возрасте 61 год.
